items.title: New players in the leptin orchestra: PNOC/NPY neurons tune appetite and obesity
items.author: Olivier Lavoie, Natalie J. Michael, Alexandre Caron
items.content: <article>Leptin regulates energy balance through the brain, yet its key neuronal targets remain unclear. Solheim et al. identify hypothalamic GABAergic PNOC/NPY neurons as mediators of leptin’s anorectic effects, advancing our understanding of neurocircuits linking adiposity signals to feeding control.</article>

items.title: Orchestrating prefrontal cognitive control: The thalamus in command
items.author: Randolph F. Helfrich
items.content: <article>In this issue of Neuron, Phillips et al. report that the thalamus encodes abstract rules, guides actions, and monitors outcomes—functions thought exclusive to the prefrontal cortex—highlighting the need to reconsider the role of thalamocortical interactions for cognitive control.</article>

items.title: Astrocytes have no CLU they contribute to Alzheimer’s disease
items.author: Lucrezia Floro, Julia TCW
items.content: <article>Clusterin (CLU) is a recognized genetic risk factor for Alzheimer’s disease. In this issue of Neuron, Lish et al. found that lower CLU levels in astrocytes, caused by the CLU risk allele, heightened inflammation and reduced synaptic functions, potentially increasing risk for cognitive decline.</article>

items.title: ACLY links mutant α-synuclein to metabolism, autophagy and neurodegeneration
items.author: Frédéric Saudou
items.content: <article>In this issue of Neuron, Son et al. reveal how pathologic α-synuclein inhibits autophagy, leading to neurodegeneration. Their work highlights the key roles of the acetyl-CoA-producing enzyme ACLY and aberrant cytoplasmic p300 acetylation, uncovering new therapeutic strategies for Parkinson’s disease.</article>

items.title: Slowing the drive: A new regulatory break for nicotine reward-related behaviors
items.author: Brandon J. Henderson
items.content: <article>In this issue of Neuron, Jehl et al. developed a “suicide” antagonist to demonstrate that the interpeduncular nucleus acts as a regulatory controller of nicotine reward-related behavior through connections with the laterodorsal tegmental nucleus.</article>
